title: Upending the super platform performance sanctuary
full_text:
Superannuation trustee accountability in the wake of the Shield and First Guardian failures has become a scalp in the war on super fund mediocrity, as the government proposes joining investment platforms to its controversial performance test.
The federal government’s latest consultation on proposed changes to the annual super performance test has received broad industry support for floating carve-outs for certain investments, including emerging or alternative asset classes and climate ventures, following years of complaints that the current test encourages benchmark hugging.
“We have heard for some time that the performance test, as it is currently written, deters and discourages investment in areas like housing and energy, venture capital and start-ups,” Jim Chalmers said on Friday.
“We don’t want the performance test to unintentionally discourage investment in some of the most important parts of our economy.” But the proposal to expand the test to super products offered on investment platforms such as HUB24 and Netwealth has split the industry, with the retail super lobby opposing the changes as industry funds push for greater scrutiny on the rapidly expanding platform market.
“The Financial Services Council is extremely cautious about proposals for expanding the test into more complex products, where test outcomes may become harder to interpret and potentially even confusing for consumers, and are less meaningful from a consumer protection standpoint,” FSC chief executive Blake Briggs said.
“Applying an investment performance and fees test to externally directed products, where consumers make decisions with professional advice, and to retirement products, where outcomes depend on individual retirement circumstances and needs, is not comparable to how the test is used for MySuper products.” The super performance test was introduced in 2021 to weed out underperformers, with a focus on mainstream default fund options, also known as MySuper products.
The test has since been expanded to include other “choice” options offered by super trustees, but externally managed products accessible on platforms are still excluded, as are retirement products. The government is now considering whether these products should be included.
The possible move comes as Treasury consults separately on increasing consumer protections in the wake of the Shield and First Guardian failures.
Shield and First Guardian were two managed investment schemes offered on platforms run by Macquarie, Equity Trustees, Netwealth and Diversa. Both collapsed in the past 18 months, leaving 12,000 people facing a total $1bn in lost super savings.
“You’ve got millions of consumers who are essentially flying blind, not knowing whether or not their super is meeting that performance benchmark that has been set for the rest of the mainstream super architecture,” Super Members Council chief executive Misha Schubert told The Australian ahead of the latest consultation.
“It’s a giant hole in consumer safety.” The council is calling for platform products to be included in the test after the prudential regulator in 2025 found that about 35 per cent of non-platform externally directed products were underperforming and were not subject to performance testing.
“The 12,000 Australians who lost their life savings in the Shield and First Guardian schemes, those products were not subject to the performance test and yet were available on four platforms and were allowed to receive compulsory tax-preferred super guarantee contributions,” Ms Schubert said.
Australia’s superannuation landscape is being redrawn in the greatest shift in a generation, as financial advisers steer billions of dollars away from industry funds as retirees become rich enough to entrust their nest eggs to wealth platforms.
The nation’s biggest industry funds, AustralianSuper, Australian Retirement Trust and Aware Super, all suffered outflows in the 2025 financial year as members exited the funds in favour of other stewards for their wealth. HUB24 and Netwealth were the biggest beneficiaries.
Association of Superannuation Funds of Australia chief executive Mary Delahunty said adding more products to the test would both level the playing field and add transparency for consumers.
“As the government recognises, the test methodology is currently flawed and unevenly applied to platforms, with only 3 per cent of platform products being included,” Ms Delahunty said.
“Reforming this, and eventually expanding the test, would be a significant change for some platform providers, but including more products on platforms would ultimately provide a more level playing field for industry, and more transparency for super fund members.
“Any extension must avoid unreasonably constraining the choice of investors who want a more hands-on role in their super.” As the retail and industry super fund lobbies clash, others have warned of potential unintended consequences from exposing super platforms to an added layer of scrutiny. “With the whole debate around consumer protections and retirement products, if that’s heavily tested on the platform side, one response might be to just create an SMSF and bypass the super platform system,” Conexus Institute executive director David Bell told The Australian.
Retail platforms offer investment products on super platforms, and also broader wealth platforms for investors. “If it gets too hard to use the superannuation version, it may lead to this pathway – and potentially we’re already starting to see it – where there’s more and more SMSFs who use the wealth platform,” Mr Bell said.
summary: The Australian government is considering expanding the superannuation performance test to include investment products on platforms, amidst industry debate over accountability and consumer protection following recent fund failures.

heading: Superannuation and Investments
key_points: The performance test may include platform products to enhance accountability after Shield and First Guardian failures.
Industry groups are divided, with retail super funds opposing the changes and industry funds advocating for greater scrutiny.
The proposed changes aim to improve transparency and consumer safety in the superannuation sector.
relevance: This is relevant to actuaries and financial services professionals as it impacts superannuation fund management, regulatory compliance, and consumer investment strategies.
